When I was building furniture back in my shop, I learned that you have to hear what the other person is saying before jumping in with advice. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've spent many years in my life, as a carpenter in Bologna, learning how to handle people and situations carefully. Now that I'm dealing with the topic of COVID vaccines—something entirely new for me—I find myself needing to be extra thoughtful and curious without being too emotional or argumentative.

Firstly, I believe it's important to listen closely when someone starts talking about this kind of thing. When I was building furniture back in my shop, I learned that you have to hear what the other person is saying before jumping in with advice. So now, if someone brings up vaccines online or asks me questions during our Sunday walk by the Arno, I try to understand where they're coming from.

I'm curious like when I first saw blueprints as a child, so I look into things myself. For instance, reading about how these vaccines work—I only have my primary school education—but understanding that my parents were both educated with master's degrees helps me respect their views if we discuss this at home or in the community center.

Being part of an agender identity means I feel more comfortable discussing topics openly, but since my neuroticism score is high on the Big Five test, I need to keep calm. My Christian faith also guides me not to spread fear unnecessarily when talking about health issues like COVID-19.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I started by reflecting on how carpentry taught patience and careful listening. Then I considered my own curiosity trait which makes me want to learn more despite limited formal education. Finally, I thought about maintaining calmness due to high neuroticism scores while aligning with religious beliefs against fear-mongering.
response_parsed.tone: Calm yet curious; respectful but straightforward.
citations: Various news sources and local health campaigns
